1:
On 1 John 2:1 — “My little children, these things write I unto you, that ye sin not. And if any man sin, we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous” — the witnesses mark the purpose against sin and the Advocate for sinners.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the balance. Robertson: “my little children” (teknia mou), tender, John an old man; “advocate” (parakletos), the same word as the Spirit in John 14:16. The Puritan Henry: he gives both dissuasion from sin and support for the fallen. The Reformed Calvin: Christ intercedes by the sacrifice of his death.

The verse states the aim and the remedy. John writes that they sin not; yet if any sin, they have an Advocate, Christ the righteous. The call to holiness and the provision for failure stand together.

So the witnesses see John, having spoken so much of sin, guard against two dangers at once: licence and despair. The witnesses note the tender address: “my little children” — the affectionate word of the aged apostle and spiritual father, used throughout the epistle. They mark the primary aim: “these things write I unto you, that ye sin not” — lest anyone take the promise of forgiveness as licence to sin, John states plainly that his whole purpose is the opposite: that they should not sin at all. They observe the gracious provision for those who do: “and if any man sin, we have an advocate with the Father” — lest the believer who falls should despair, John points to the Advocate, the parakletos, one called alongside to plead our cause; Christ stands in the Father’s presence as our defender. They note the name that grounds his pleading: “Jesus Christ the righteous” — he is fit to be our Advocate precisely because he is righteous, having no fault of his own and a perfect righteousness to present. They observe that Calvin grounds the intercession in the sacrifice of his death: Christ pleads on the basis of his finished atonement. They note the balance Henry marks: dissuasion from sin and support for the fallen, held together. The verse thus sets the double truth: John writes that they sin not, yet provides for failure in the righteous Advocate — whose propitiation the next verse declares.
2:
On 1 John 2:2 — “And he is the propitiation for our sins: and not for ours only, but also for the sins of the whole world” — the witnesses mark Christ the propitiation, and a great debate on its extent.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “the propitiation” (hilasmos), only here and 1 John 4:10; Christ both priest and sacrifice (Hebrews 2:17). Adam Clarke: the atoning sacrifice for our sins. The Reformed Calvin: the expiation extends to all who by faith embrace the gospel; he passes by the dream of universal salvation. Wesley: “just as wide as sin extends, the propitiation extends also.”

The verse names Christ the propitiation. He atones for our sins, and for the whole world. The extent of that atonement is debated.

So the witnesses see John ground the Advocate’s pleading in his propitiation, and extend it beyond the immediate circle. The witnesses note the great term: “he is the propitiation for our sins” — Christ is himself the atoning sacrifice that appeases God’s wrath and covers sin; he is, as Robertson observes, both priest and victim, the very means of pardon. They mark the sweeping extension: “and not for ours only, but also for the sins of the whole world” — the propitiation reaches beyond the apostles, beyond believing Jews, beyond any narrow circle. They observe that the precise extent of this “whole world” is one of the classic divisions among the witnesses. The Reformed, with Calvin, take it that the expiation is sufficient for all and extends to all who by faith embrace the gospel, of every nation — Calvin expressly setting aside the notion of universal salvation of every individual. The Arminian, with Wesley, reads it more broadly: the propitiation is as wide as sin itself, made for all men, though effectual only in those who believe. They note that both sides agree Christ’s sacrifice is not for one people only but for the world, Gentile as well as Jew. They observe that the verse comforts the believer: the Advocate pleads a propitiation of boundless sufficiency. The verse thus crowns the comfort of the Advocate: Christ is the propitiation for our sins and for the whole world — the ground of all forgiveness, whose application John tests by obedience in the verses following.
3:
On 1 John 2:3 — “And hereby we do know that we know him, if we keep his commandments” — the witnesses mark obedience as the test of knowing God.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the test. Robertson: “hereby” (en touto), explained by the “if” clause, “if we keep on keeping his commandments”; a favorite Johannine phrase. Adam Clarke: keeping the commandments is the fullest proof of saving knowledge. The Reformed Calvin: the knowledge of God from the gospel is not ineffectual. Wesley: we know him savingly as Advocate, righteous, propitiation.

The verse gives the test of true knowledge. We know that we know God if we keep his commandments. Obedience is the proof of acquaintance with God.

So the witnesses see John turn from doctrine to its practical test: the knowledge of God is proved by obedience. The witnesses note the assurance offered: “hereby we do know that we know him” — there is a way to be sure that our claim to know God is real; John gives a test of genuine acquaintance with God. They mark the test: “if we keep his commandments” — the present tense marking continual obedience; the keeping of God’s commandments is the evidence that one truly knows him. They observe that this strikes directly at the error of the false teachers, who (as Clarke notes) boasted great knowledge of God while disregarding his commands; John makes obedience, not mere profession or speculation, the proof. They note Calvin’s point that the knowledge of God drawn from the gospel is not idle or ineffectual but transforms the life; a true knowledge of God necessarily produces obedience. They observe that this is no salvation by works, but works as the fruit and evidence of faith: the obedient life shows that the knowledge of God is genuine. They note Wesley’s sense that to know him savingly — as Advocate, righteous, and propitiation — is to keep his word. The verse thus lays down the great test of the epistle: we know that we know God if we keep his commandments — a test that, the next verse warns, unmasks the disobedient professor as a liar.
4:
On 1 John 2:4 — “He that saith, I know him, and keepeth not his commandments, is a liar, and the truth is not in him” — the witnesses mark the unmasking of the disobedient professor.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rebuke. Robertson: “I know him” (egnoka auton), one of the pious platitudes of the Gnostics, punctured by John’s blunt “liar.” Adam Clarke: a severe blow against pretenders who live in sin, and against the Antinomians. The Reformed Calvin: those who boast faith without piety are liars.

The verse exposes the false claim. He who claims to know God but disobeys is a liar. Profession without obedience is falsehood.

So the witnesses see John apply the test of verse 3 to expose the hollow professor. The witnesses note the claim: “he that saith, I know him” — one who professes acquaintance with God, perhaps with the confident boast of superior knowledge that marked the false teachers; Robertson calls it one of their pious platitudes. They mark the fatal contradiction: “and keepeth not his commandments” — yet does not obey God; the profession of knowing him is belied by a disobedient life. They observe the blunt verdict: “is a liar, and the truth is not in him” — John does not soften it; such a man lies, and the truth has no place in him; his claim is simply false. They note that this strikes, as Clarke says, a severe blow against all pretenders to religious knowledge who live in sin, and against the Antinomian who would divorce knowing Christ from obeying him. They observe Calvin’s point that those who boast faith or knowledge without piety are convicted of falsehood by their own conduct. They note the sharpness of John’s either/or: there is no knowing God that leaves the commandments unkept. The verse thus unmasks the disobedient professor: to claim knowledge of God while breaking his commandments is to be a liar void of the truth — against which the next verse sets the one who keeps his word.

So the witnesses see John apply the test of verse 3 to expose the hollow professor. The claim: “he that saith, I know him” — one who professes acquaintance with God, perhaps with the confident boast of superior knowledge that marked the false teachers; Robertson calls it one of their pious platitudes. The fatal contradiction: “and keepeth not his commandments” — the profession belied by a disobedient life. The blunt verdict: “is a liar, and the truth is not in him” — John does not soften it. This strikes, as Clarke says, a severe blow against all pretenders to religious knowledge who live in sin, and against the Antinomian who would divorce knowing Christ from obeying him. There is no knowing God that leaves the commandments unkept.
5:
On 1 John 2:5 — “But whoso keepeth his word, in him verily is the love of God perfected: hereby know we that we are in him” — the witnesses mark the love of God perfected in the obedient.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the assurance. Robertson: “whoso keepeth” (hos d’ an tere), indefinite, open to all, not a few Gnostic initiates; “verily” (alethos), truly. Adam Clarke: the design of God’s love is accomplished. The Reformed Calvin: to love God in sincerity of heart is to keep his word. Wesley: so is the tree known by its fruits.

The verse states the positive side. In the one who keeps God’s word the love of God is perfected. Obedience is the proof of being in him.

So the witnesses see John set the true believer against the false professor: in the obedient, God’s love is perfected. The witnesses note the contrast: “but whoso keepeth his word” — over against the liar who keeps not the commandments, here is the one who truly keeps God’s word; Robertson notes the indefinite “whoso,” the prize open to all, not reserved for a few initiates. They mark the great declaration: “in him verily is the love of God perfected” — in the obedient believer the love of God reaches its intended completion. They observe the expositors’ readings: it may mean God’s love to us attains its purpose and full effect in the obedient life; or, as Calvin takes it, that to keep God’s word is itself the perfecting of our love to God, love proving itself in obedience. They note that either way, obedience and love are inseparable: real love for God shows itself in keeping his word, and where his word is kept, his love has done its perfect work. They observe the assurance drawn: “hereby know we that we are in him” — the keeping of his word gives the believer evidence that he is truly united to God; Wesley adds, the tree is known by its fruits. They note that this turns the test into comfort: obedience assures the soul of its union with God. The verse thus crowns the test of knowledge with the test of love: in the one who keeps God’s word the love of God is perfected, and by it we know we are in him — a union the next verse says must show itself in walking as Christ walked.
6:
On 1 John 2:6 — “He that saith he abideth in him ought himself also so to walk, even as he walked” — the witnesses mark the call to walk as Christ walked.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the obligation. Robertson: “to walk” (peripatein), a continuous performance, not a spasmodic spurt; “even as he walked” (constative aorist of Christ’s life). Adam Clarke: prove the profession by walking as Christ walked. The Reformed Calvin: from our union with Christ he proves we ought to be like him.

The verse draws the practical consequence. He who claims to abide in Christ must walk as Christ walked. Union with Christ obliges to imitation of Christ.

So the witnesses see John press the highest claim — abiding in Christ — to its practical obligation. The witnesses note the profession: “he that saith he abideth in him” — one who claims the intimate, lasting union with Christ, to remain and dwell in him. They mark the obligation that claim entails: “ought himself also so to walk, even as he walked” — such a one is bound to order his whole manner of life after the pattern of Christ’s own; the word “walk,” as Robertson notes, means a continuous course of life, not occasional good acts. They observe the standard: not merely to obey commandments in general, but to walk “even as he walked” — to take Christ’s own earthly life of love, holiness, and obedience as the model. They note Calvin’s logic that John does not simply exhort to imitate Christ, but argues from the union itself: because we are joined to him, we ought to be like him; likeness of life is the proof and fruit of the union. They observe that this is the loftiest of the tests: the claim to abide in Christ is verified by a Christlike walk. They note that profession of union with Christ that does not issue in imitation of Christ is empty. The verse thus crowns the section on obedience: whoever claims to abide in Christ must walk as Christ walked — before John turns to the heart of that walk, the commandment of love.
7:
On 1 John 2:7 — “Brethren, I write no new commandment unto you, but an old commandment which ye had from the beginning” — the witnesses mark the old commandment of love.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “beloved” (agapetoi), the first instance of this address; “no new commandment” (kainen, novel in kind), the old one had “from the beginning.” Adam Clarke notes the seeming contradiction with verse 8. The Puritan Henry: walking as Christ walked is no new but an old commandment.

The verse calls the commandment old. It is no new commandment but one held from the beginning. The duty of love is ancient.

So the witnesses see John introduce the commandment of love as one already familiar. The witnesses note the affectionate address “brethren” (or “beloved”), the first such in the epistle, fitting for a charge about love. They mark the denial of novelty: “I write no new commandment unto you” — the commandment he is about to press, brotherly love, is not some strange new doctrine. They observe its antiquity: “but an old commandment which ye had from the beginning” — it is the commandment they had heard from the first of their Christian instruction, indeed (the expositors note) rooted in the ancient law of love and taught from the beginning of the gospel. They note that John guards against the suspicion of novelty, which men distrust; he is not innovating but recalling them to a duty they have always known. They observe the apparent contradiction with the next verse, which calls it “new”; the expositors resolve it by the different senses — old in its origin and teaching, new in the fresh force and example Christ gave it. They note that the commandment of love is thus both venerable and ever-fresh. The verse thus presents the commandment as old: the love John urges is no novelty but the ancient commandment held from the beginning — which, the next verse adds, is yet new in Christ and in them.

So the witnesses see John introduce the commandment of love as one already familiar. The affectionate address “brethren” (or “beloved”) is the first such in the epistle, fitting for a charge about love. The denial of novelty: “I write no new commandment” — the brotherly love he presses is not some strange new doctrine. Its antiquity: “an old commandment which ye had from the beginning” — heard from the first of their instruction, rooted in the ancient law of love. John guards against the suspicion of novelty, which men distrust; he is not innovating but recalling them to a duty always known. The apparent contradiction with the next verse resolves by sense: old in origin and teaching, new in the fresh force Christ gave it.
8:
On 1 John 2:8 — “Again, a new commandment I write unto you, which thing is true in him and in you: because the darkness is past, and the true light now shineth” — the witnesses mark the commandment as also new.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the paradox. Robertson: “a new commandment” (kainen) — old in teaching (as old as Cain and Abel), but new in practice; cf. John 13:34. Adam Clarke: true in him — Christ loved the world; true in you — ready to lay down your lives. The Reformed Calvin: God renews it daily that the faithful may practice it.

The verse calls the same commandment new. It is true in Christ and in them, for the light now shines. The old commandment is made new in the gospel.

So the witnesses see John complete the paradox: the old commandment is also a new one. The witnesses note the seeming reversal: “again, a new commandment I write unto you” — the very commandment just called old he now calls new; the expositors resolve the paradox by sense, as Robertson puts it: old in teaching, but new in practice and in the fresh form Christ gave it (“a new commandment I give unto you, that ye love one another”). They mark the ground of its newness: “which thing is true in him and in you” — the commandment of love is realized and verified, true and operative, both in Christ (who supremely fulfilled it in loving and dying for us) and in believers (in whom that love is now reproduced). They observe the reason given: “because the darkness is past, and the true light now shineth” — with the coming of Christ, the true Light, the old darkness of ignorance and sin is passing away, and in that new light the commandment of love takes on fresh power and clarity. They note Calvin’s sense that God renews this commandment daily, that the faithful may practice it through their whole life. They observe that love is thus both ancient and ever-new: as old as God’s law, yet made new by Christ’s example and the dawning gospel light. The verse thus completes the paradox: the commandment of love is new, true in Christ and in believers, because the true light now shines — the light against which, the next verses warn, the hater of his brother stands convicted of darkness.
9:
On 1 John 2:9 — “He that saith he is in the light, and hateth his brother, is in darkness even until now” — the witnesses mark hatred as the proof of darkness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contradiction. Robertson: the claim to be in the light is nullified by hating one’s brother; the only way to walk in the light is fellowship with God who is light. Adam Clarke: he who professes Christianity yet hates his brother has received no saving light. The Reformed Calvin: love is the rule by which our life is formed.

The verse exposes the loveless professor. He who claims the light yet hates his brother is in darkness still. Hatred belies the profession of light.

So the witnesses see John make brotherly love the test of being in the light. The witnesses note the claim: “he that saith he is in the light” — one who professes to be enlightened, a Christian, in fellowship with the God who is light. They mark the contradicting reality: “and hateth his brother” — yet harbors hatred toward his fellow-believer; the absence of love, indeed positive ill-will, toward a brother. They observe the verdict: “is in darkness even until now” — such a one, for all his claim, remains in the very darkness he professed to have left; his hatred proves he was never truly in the light. They note Robertson’s point that the claim to be in the light is simply nullified by hating one’s brother, since the only way to walk in the light is fellowship with the God who is light and love. They observe Clarke’s sharpness: he who professes Christianity yet hates his brother has received no saving illumination at all. They note Calvin’s sense that love is the very rule by which the Christian life is shaped; where it is absent, the profession is hollow. They observe that this parallels the earlier tests: as disobedience unmasked the false claim to know God, so hatred unmasks the false claim to be in the light. The verse thus exposes the loveless professor: to claim the light while hating one’s brother is to be in darkness still — against which the next verse sets the one who loves.
10:
On 1 John 2:10 — “He that loveth his brother abideth in the light, and there is none occasion of stumbling in him” — the witnesses mark love as the mark of abiding in light.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrast. Robertson: “abideth” (menei), continues in the light, not interrupting it by hatred; “occasion of stumbling” (skandalon), a stumbling-block. Adam Clarke: he that loves his brother gives proof he has received Christ. The Reformed Calvin: love toward the brethren proves we love God.

The verse states the positive mark. He who loves his brother abides in the light, with no stumbling-block in him. Love is the evidence of true light.

So the witnesses see John set the loving believer against the hating professor. The witnesses note the mark: “he that loveth his brother” — the one who genuinely loves his fellow-believer, and by love serves him; this love is the touchstone. They mark the result: “abideth in the light” — such a one continues, remains, in the light of God; his love does not interrupt or extinguish the light, as hatred does, but keeps him walking in it. They observe the further blessing: “and there is none occasion of stumbling in him” — the word means a stumbling-block or trap; the expositors take it either that there is nothing in him to make others fall, or nothing to cause his own fall; love removes the stumbling-blocks that hatred sets up, both in himself and toward others. They note Calvin’s point that love to the brethren is the testimony by which we prove we love God; the two cannot be separated. They observe Clarke’s sense that he who loves his brother gives proof that he has truly received Christ. They note that love is thus the great evidence of being in the light, as hatred is the proof of darkness. The verse thus declares the positive mark: the one who loves his brother abides in the light, free of stumbling — in sharp contrast, the next verse adds, with the blinded walk of the hater.

So the witnesses see John set the loving believer against the hating professor. The mark: “he that loveth his brother” — the one who genuinely loves his fellow-believer is the touchstone. The result: “abideth in the light” — such a one continues in God’s light, his love not interrupting it as hatred does. The further blessing: “there is none occasion of stumbling in him” — the word means a stumbling-block; either there is nothing in him to make others fall, or nothing to cause his own fall; love removes the stumbling-blocks hatred sets up. Calvin’s point: love to the brethren is the testimony by which we prove we love God; the two cannot be separated. Love is the great evidence of being in the light.
11:
On 1 John 2:11 — “But he that hateth his brother is in darkness, and walketh in darkness, and knoweth not whither he goeth, because that darkness hath blinded his eyes” — the witnesses mark the blinding power of hatred.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “blinded” (etyphlosen), the same verb used in 2 Corinthians 4:4 of Satan blinding minds. Adam Clarke: still in his unconverted state, knowing not whither he goes. Wesley: in sin, perplexity, entanglement, in the high road to hell.

The verse pictures the hater’s blindness. He is in darkness, walks in darkness, and is blinded so he knows not where he goes. Hatred darkens and disorients the soul.

So the witnesses see John complete the contrast by painting the hater’s threefold plight in darkness. The witnesses note the accumulating description: “he that hateth his brother is in darkness” — his state is darkness; “and walketh in darkness” — his conduct, his whole course of life, is in darkness; the inward state issues in an outward walk. They mark the disorientation: “and knoweth not whither he goeth” — like a man in the dark, he has lost his way, ignorant of his true direction and destiny; as Wesley grimly adds, he walks on, not knowing he is in the high road to hell. They observe the cause: “because that darkness hath blinded his eyes” — the darkness of hatred has not merely surrounded him but blinded him, robbing him of spiritual sight; Robertson notes the very verb used elsewhere of Satan blinding the minds of unbelievers. They note the terrible progression: hatred is darkness, breeds a dark walk, and blinds the soul to its own peril. They observe that this is the opposite of the loving believer who abides in the light and does not stumble: the hater stumbles because he cannot see. They note that love and hatred are thus revealed as light and darkness, sight and blindness. The verse thus seals the contrast: the hater of his brother walks blinded in darkness, not knowing his way — before John turns from warning to the assurances he addresses to his readers by their stages of growth.
12:
On 1 John 2:12 — “I write unto you, little children, because your sins are forgiven you for his name’s sake” — the witnesses mark the ground of assurance: forgiveness.

The Latin fathers note the aim. Augustine: those things must be commended to memory which are of most force against heretics. The grammatical-historical Robertson: “I write” (present), “for the name” (3 John 1:7); their sins forgiven. The Reformed Calvin: “little children” here means men of all ages, not only the young. Wesley: forgiveness the common privilege of all.

The verse begins the addresses by stages. John writes to the “little children” because their sins are forgiven. Forgiveness is the foundation of all.

So the witnesses see John turn from warning to a series of warm assurances, addressing his readers under different names. The witnesses note the first address: “I write unto you, little children” — the expositors debate whether this names the youngest believers or, as Calvin holds, the whole family of God of every age (the same tender word John uses for all his readers); here, in the threefold scheme, it likely heads the list as the general or youngest class. They mark the ground given: “because your sins are forgiven you for his name’s sake” — the first and most basic assurance of the gospel, that their sins are pardoned; and the ground of that pardon is “for his name’s sake” — on account of Christ, his merit and atoning work, not their own worth. They observe that John writes not to terrify but to assure: his readers are forgiven people, and on that foundation all else rests. They note Augustine’s reminder that such truths, especially against heretics, must be fixed in memory. They observe that the pardon of sin for Christ’s sake is the bedrock of Christian confidence. The verse thus opens the section of assurances: John writes to the little children because their sins are forgiven for Christ’s name’s sake — the foundational comfort he will build on as he addresses fathers and young men.

So the witnesses see John turn from warning to a series of warm assurances. The first address: “I write unto you, little children” — the expositors debate whether this names the youngest believers or, as Calvin holds, the whole family of God of every age; here it likely heads the threefold list. The ground given: “because your sins are forgiven you for his name’s sake” — the first and most basic assurance of the gospel, pardon of sin; and its ground is “for his name’s sake,” on account of Christ and his atoning work, not their own worth. John writes not to terrify but to assure: his readers are a forgiven people, and on that foundation all else rests. The pardon of sin for Christ’s sake is the bedrock of Christian confidence.
13:
On 1 John 2:13 — “I write unto you, fathers… young men… little children, because ye have known the Father” — the witnesses mark the addresses to the stages of spiritual growth.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the three classes. Robertson: “fathers” the mature with long experience (“him from the beginning”); “young men” who have overcome the wicked one; “little children” who know the Father. The Reformed Calvin: he enumerates ages to show his teaching suits every one. Wesley details the scheme of fathers, young men, little children.

The verse addresses the stages of growth. Fathers know the eternal One; young men have overcome the evil one; children know the Father. Each stage has its assurance.

So the witnesses see John address his readers according to their stages of spiritual maturity, each with its fitting assurance. The witnesses note the “fathers”: “because ye have known him that is from the beginning” — the mature believers, rich in long experience, are commended for their deep, settled knowledge of the eternal Christ, the Word who was from the beginning; theirs is the seasoned knowledge of God himself. They mark the “young men”: “because ye have overcome the wicked one” — those in the vigor of spiritual life are commended for victory in the conflict, having overcome Satan; theirs is strength and triumph in the battle. They observe the “little children” (using a different word here): “because ye have known the Father” — the youngest believers are commended for that first, precious knowledge by which they can call God Father. They note Calvin’s point that John enumerates the ages to show that his teaching fits every stage, lest any think the message not meant for them. They observe the wisdom of suiting the assurance to the stage: maturity has its deep knowledge, youth its victory, infancy its filial knowledge of the Father. They note that all are addressed as those who truly belong to God. The verse thus assures each class: fathers who know the eternal One, young men who have conquered the wicked one, children who know the Father — assurances John repeats and amplifies in the next verse.
14:
On 1 John 2:14 — “I have written unto you, fathers… young men, because ye are strong, and the word of God abideth in you, and ye have overcome the wicked one” — the witnesses mark the repeated and amplified assurances.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the repetition. Robertson: “I have written” (epistolary aorist), repeated three times; perhaps John resumes after an interruption. Adam Clarke: the word of God moulded into their hearts, the abiding testimony of the Spirit. The Reformed Calvin thinks the repetitions for amplification.

The verse repeats the addresses, enlarging the charge to the young men. They are strong, the word abides in them, they have overcome. Strength and the abiding word secure the victory.

So the witnesses see John repeat his addresses, now amplifying especially the young men. The witnesses note the repetition: the fathers are addressed again with the same ground, “ye have known him that is from the beginning” — their mature knowledge needs no addition, John speaking to them briefly and reverently. They mark the fuller word to the young men: “because ye are strong, and the word of God abideth in you, and ye have overcome the wicked one” — their victory (already named) is now explained by its source. They observe the threefold ground of their triumph: they are “strong” — vigorous in spiritual life; “the word of God abideth in you” — the truth has taken deep, permanent root in their hearts, moulding them; and so “ye have overcome the wicked one” — by that indwelling word they have conquered Satan. They note Clarke’s point that the word of God is not merely known but moulded into their very hearts, the abiding testimony of the Spirit. They observe that the secret of the young men’s victory is the abiding word: spiritual strength comes from the truth dwelling within, and by it the tempter is overcome. They note the comfort that the conflict, fierce in the young, is already a victory through the word. The verse thus amplifies the assurances: the young men are strong and victorious because the word of God abides in them — a strength John will at once direct away from the love of the world.
15:
On 1 John 2:15 — “Love not the world, neither the things that are in the world. If any man love the world, the love of the Father is not in him” — the witnesses mark the prohibition of worldly love.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “love not the world” (prohibition, stop or do not have the habit); kosmos as the evil world-system (cf. 1 John 5:19). The Puritan Henry: the world draws the heart from God. The Reformed Calvin: the vain love of the world must be torn away. Wesley: if any seek happiness in visible things, he loves not God.

The verse forbids the love of the world. To love the world is to lack the love of the Father. The two loves are mutually exclusive.

So the witnesses see John, having assured the young men of victory, direct that strength against the love of the world. The witnesses note the prohibition: “love not the world, neither the things that are in the world” — the “world” here meaning not the physical creation, nor people as objects of compassion, but the evil world-system, the sphere of sin and vanity opposed to God, with its allurements; the present prohibition bids them stop, or never form the habit of, loving it. They mark the absolute incompatibility: “if any man love the world, the love of the Father is not in him” — the two loves cannot coexist; to set the heart’s supreme affection on the world is proof that the love of the Father does not dwell there. They observe Wesley’s sharp restatement: whoever seeks his happiness in visible, perishing things does not love God. They note Henry’s point that the world draws the heart away from God; the warning guards the affections. They observe that this is no call to despise God’s creatures rightly used, but to refuse the inordinate love that makes the world one’s portion and idol. They note the link to the victory just named: the young men’s strength is to be spent overcoming the world, not loving it. The verse thus lays down the great prohibition: love not the world, for love of the world excludes love of the Father — the reason for which the next verse unfolds.
16:
On 1 John 2:16 — “For all that is in the world, the lust of the flesh, and the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life, is not of the Father, but is of the world” — the witnesses mark the threefold analysis of worldliness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the three. Robertson: three examples in apposition — “the lust of the flesh,” “the lust of the eyes,” “the vainglory of life” (alazoneia). Wesley: the desire of the flesh (the outward senses), the desire of the eye (the imagination), the pride of life. The Reformed Calvin: whatever is in the world is not of God.

The verse analyzes the world’s appeal. It is the lust of the flesh, the lust of the eyes, and the pride of life — all not of the Father. Worldliness is sensuality, covetousness, and pride.

So the witnesses see John dissect the world’s allurement into its three great forms. The witnesses note the comprehensive claim: “all that is in the world” — the sum of what the world offers to seduce the heart — falls under three heads. They mark the first: “the lust of the flesh” — the craving for sensual gratification, the pleasures of the bodily appetites, whether of taste, touch, or indulgence. They observe the second: “the lust of the eyes” — the desire stirred through the sight, covetousness, the love of show, riches, and beautiful things grasped for their own sake; Wesley relates it to the pleasures of the imagination. They note the third: “the pride of life” — the vainglory and ostentation of living, the boastful self-display, ambition, and pomp of worldly status. They observe that these three together compass the world’s appeal, and some have seen in them the very pattern of the first temptation and of Christ’s temptation — appetite, beauty, and pride. They mark the decisive verdict: “is not of the Father, but is of the world” — none of these springs from God or bears his stamp; they belong wholly to the fallen world-system. They note that to love these is therefore to love what is not of the Father. The verse thus exposes the anatomy of worldliness — fleshly lust, covetous eyes, and proud display, all from the world, not the Father — the world whose passing the next verse contrasts with the eternal.
17:
On 1 John 2:17 — “And the world passeth away, and the lust thereof: but he that doeth the will of God abideth for ever” — the witnesses mark the passing world and the abiding doer of God’s will.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrast. Robertson: “passeth away” (paragetai, is passing by, linear); consolation in the transitoriness of the conflict. Adam Clarke: all these things are continually fading and perishing. The Reformed Calvin: they who seek happiness from the fading world make wretched provision. Wesley: he that loves God abideth in the enjoyment of what he loves for ever.

The verse weighs the world against eternity. The world and its lust pass away, but the doer of God’s will abides forever. The transient world is no fit object of love.

So the witnesses see John clinch the warning against the world by setting its transience against the permanence of the godly. The witnesses note the great contrast’s first term: “the world passeth away, and the lust thereof” — the whole world-system, with all its objects of desire, is even now passing by, fading and perishing; the present tense, as Robertson notes, pictures it as already in the act of slipping away. They mark the folly this exposes: to love the world is to set the heart on what is vanishing; as Calvin says, they who seek their happiness from so fleeting a thing make wretched and miserable provision for themselves. They observe the second term: “but he that doeth the will of God abideth for ever” — in sharp contrast, the one who does God’s will, who loves and obeys God rather than the world, endures forever, sharing the permanence of the God he serves. They note Wesley’s point that such a one abides in the eternal enjoyment of what he loves — God himself. They observe the decisive argument: why love the world, which cannot last, when one may do God’s will and abide forever? They note that the doer of God’s will is set against the lover of the world as the eternal against the perishing. The verse thus crowns the warning: the world and its lust pass away, but the doer of God’s will abides for ever — before John turns to the perilous “last time” and its antichrists.
18:
On 1 John 2:18 — “Little children, it is the last time: and as ye have heard that antichrist shall come, even now are there many antichrists; whereby we know that it is the last time” — the witnesses mark the last hour and its antichrists.

The Latin fathers note the urgency. Augustine: he bids the children make haste to grow, “because it is the last hour.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “the last hour” (eschate hora), marking the character of the hour. Adam Clarke: variously understood; the last dispensation, or the close of the Jewish state. Wesley: under antichrist he includes all false teachers and enemies of Christ.

The verse announces the last time. As antichrist was foretold, now many antichrists have come, proving it the last hour. The presence of antichrists marks the age.

So the witnesses see John warn of the perilous character of the age by the rise of antichrists. The witnesses note the solemn announcement: “it is the last time” (or “the last hour”) — the final period of redemptive history, the last dispensation that runs to the end; the expositors differ on the exact sense (the last age of the world, or the closing of the Jewish state with Jerusalem’s fall near), but agree it marks a final, critical hour. They mark the sign of it: “as ye have heard that antichrist shall come” — the readers had been taught that a great antichrist, the climactic enemy of Christ, was to appear. They observe John’s point: “even now are there many antichrists” — already many lesser antichrists, false teachers who deny and oppose Christ, had arisen; Wesley takes “antichrist” broadly for all such enemies and seducers. They note the inference: “whereby we know that it is the last time” — the swarming of these antichrists is itself the proof that the last hour has come. They observe Augustine’s urgent application: since it is the last hour, the children must make haste to grow. They note that John writes to fortify the faithful against the disturbance these errorists caused. The verse thus sounds the alarm of the age: it is the last hour, and the many antichrists prove it — errorists whose origin, the next verse explains, was from within the professing church.
19:
On 1 John 2:19 — “They went out from us, but they were not of us; for if they had been of us, they would no doubt have continued with us” — the witnesses mark the apostasy of the false teachers.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the two senses. Robertson: “from us” (they went out from our membership) and “of us” (they were not of us in spirit and life) — the same idiom in different senses. Adam Clarke: they professed Christianity but were never commissioned by the apostles. The Reformed Calvin: it disturbs the weak when one among us falls away. Wesley: their hearts had departed before they went.

The verse explains the antichrists’ origin. They left the church, showing they were never truly of it. Apostasy reveals the heart that was never genuine.

So the witnesses see John explain the troubling fact that these antichrists had come from within the church. The witnesses note the painful admission: “they went out from us” — the false teachers had once been members of the Christian community, professed believers among them; their defection from the church was a real grief. They mark the crucial distinction John draws: “but they were not of us” — though they had belonged outwardly, they had never truly belonged in heart and life; the same phrase shifts sense, as Robertson notes, from membership to genuine spiritual union. They observe the proof: “for if they had been of us, they would no doubt have continued with us” — true belonging would have shown itself in perseverance; their falling away demonstrates that they were never genuinely Christ’s. They note Wesley’s point that their hearts had departed before their feet did; the outward going-out only manifested an inward estrangement long present. They observe the purpose John adds: “that they might be made manifest that they were not all of us” — the apostasy served to unmask the false. They note Calvin’s pastoral concern that such defections disturb the weak, who are steadied by understanding that the apostate was never truly of the body. They observe the comfort: the church is not discredited by those who leave it, for they were never really of it. The verse thus accounts for the antichrists: they went out because they were never truly of us — their departure manifesting the difference, while the readers, the next verse assures, have the abiding anointing.
20:
On 1 John 2:20 — “But ye have an unction from the Holy One, and ye know all things” — the witnesses mark the believers’ anointing.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the term. Robertson: “anointing” (chrisma), the result and material of anointing, perhaps suggested by “antichrist”; Christians are “anointed ones.” Adam Clarke: an allusion to the holy anointing oil. The Puritan Henry: their stability in the day of seducers. Wesley: an inward teaching from the Holy Ghost, whereby ye know all things needful.

The verse assures the believers of their anointing. They have an unction from the Holy One and know all things. The Spirit’s teaching guards them from error.

So the witnesses see John turn from the antichrists to the safeguard of the true believers: the anointing. The witnesses note the contrast: against the seducers stands the assurance, “but ye have an unction from the Holy One” — believers possess an anointing, a chrisma, given by the Holy One (Christ, or God); the word, Robertson notes, may be set deliberately against “antichrist,” for Christians are the truly “anointed ones.” They mark that this anointing is the Holy Spirit and his grace, given to believers as Christ the Anointed received the Spirit without measure. They observe the effect: “and ye know all things” — by this inward teaching of the Spirit they have a sufficient knowledge of the truth, enough (as Wesley qualifies) to preserve them from the seducers and for their salvation; not omniscience, but the discernment the Spirit gives. They note Henry’s point that this is their stability in the dangerous day of seducers: the indwelling Spirit keeps them from being deceived. They observe that the anointing distinguishes the true from the false: the antichrists went out, but the anointed remain, taught of God. They note that this is the believer’s great security against error. The verse thus comforts the readers: they have the Spirit’s anointing from the Holy One and know the truth — a knowledge for which, the next verse explains, John writes not to inform but to confirm them.

So the witnesses see John turn from the antichrists to the safeguard of the true believers: the anointing. Against the seducers stands the assurance, “ye have an unction from the Holy One” — believers possess an anointing, a chrisma, given by the Holy One; the word, Robertson notes, may be set against “antichrist,” for Christians are the truly “anointed ones.” This anointing is the Holy Spirit and his grace. The effect: “ye know all things” — by the Spirit’s inward teaching they have sufficient knowledge of the truth, enough (Wesley qualifies) to preserve them from the seducers and for salvation, not omniscience but the discernment the Spirit gives. This is their stability in the dangerous day of seducers: the indwelling Spirit keeps them from being deceived.
21:
On 1 John 2:21 — “I have not written unto you because ye know not the truth, but because ye know it, and that no lie is of the truth” — the witnesses mark John’s respect for their knowledge.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the courtesy. Robertson: not the epistolary aorist but a reference to what he has just said; whether hoti is causal or declarative. Adam Clarke: he writes not because they are ignorant but because they know, and can judge the false teachers. The Reformed Calvin: he concedes to them a judgment to distinguish truth from falsehood.

The verse states why John writes. Not because they lack the truth, but because they know it. He confirms, not instructs, their knowledge.

So the witnesses see John graciously explain that his writing assumes, rather than doubts, his readers’ knowledge. The witnesses note the courteous disclaimer: “I have not written unto you because ye know not the truth” — John does not want his earnest warnings taken as an insult, as though he reckoned them ignorant; he is careful, like Paul to the Romans, not to seem to reprove their understanding. They mark the true reason: “but because ye know it” — he writes precisely because they do know the truth, to confirm and stir up the knowledge they already have, and to enable them to apply it against the seducers. They observe the added ground: “and that no lie is of the truth” — they know not only the truth but this corollary, that no falsehood can spring from or agree with the truth; truth and lie are utterly opposed. They note Calvin’s point that John concedes them a real power of judgment, by which they can discern truth from error — the practical discernment the anointing gives, not mere school-learning. They observe that this both honors the readers and equips them: knowing the truth, and that no lie belongs to it, they can detect the antichrists’ lies. They note that John writes to a discerning people. The verse thus frames his purpose: he writes not to the ignorant but to those who know the truth and that no lie is of it — a knowledge that arms them against the specific lie the next verse names.
22:
On 1 John 2:22 — “Who is a liar but he that denieth that Jesus is the Christ? He is antichrist, that denieth the Father and the Son” — the witnesses mark the doctrine of antichrist.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the denial. Robertson: “the liar” (with the article); a rhetorical question; “that denieth that Jesus is the Christ.” Adam Clarke: certain heretics acknowledged Jesus a teacher but denied he was the Christ. The Eastern fathers Cyril: the Jews allow he is Jesus but not the Christ; hence the apostle’s word. Wesley: that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, come in the flesh, is one undivided truth.

The verse defines the antichrist. The liar is he who denies Jesus is the Christ, denying the Father and the Son. To deny the Son is the antichristian lie.

So the witnesses see John name the specific lie that marks the antichrist. The witnesses note the pointed question: “who is a liar but he that denieth that Jesus is the Christ?” — the great lie, the falsehood above all others, is the denial that Jesus is the Messiah, the Christ. They mark the historical reference the expositors supply: certain early heretics (such as Cerinthus and the Gnostics) acknowledged Jesus as a man or teacher but denied that he was truly the Christ, the Son of God come in the flesh; the Jews, Cyril notes, allow he is Jesus but deny he is the Christ. They observe the grave name: “he is antichrist, that denieth the Father and the Son” — such a denier is the antichrist in spirit; for to deny that Jesus is the Christ, the Son, is to deny the Son, and so also the Father, since the two are inseparably joined. They note Wesley’s point that “Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, come in the flesh” is one undivided truth, and to deny any part is to deny the whole. They observe that John fixes the test of orthodoxy on the person of Christ: the antichrist is known by his denial of the Son. They note that this is the lie the anointing teaches believers to detect. The verse thus identifies the antichrist by his doctrine: he denies that Jesus is the Christ, and so denies the Father and the Son — a denial whose fatal consequence the next verse states.
23:
On 1 John 2:23 — “Whosoever denieth the Son, the same hath not the Father: but he that acknowledgeth the Son hath the Father also” — the witnesses mark the inseparability of the Father and the Son.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the axiom. Robertson: “hath not the Father” — the Son reveals the Father (John 1:18; John 14:9); our only approach to the Father is by the Son (John 14:6). The Reformed Calvin: a remarkable sentence, among the first axioms of religion. The Eastern fathers Cyril: he that denieth the Son hath not the Father; no one comes to the Father but through Christ.

The verse states the great axiom. To deny the Son is to lose the Father; to confess the Son is to have the Father. There is no God apart from Christ.

So the witnesses see John lay down a foundational axiom of the faith: the Father is had only through the Son. The witnesses note the first half: “whosoever denieth the Son, the same hath not the Father” — the one who rejects Christ, denying that Jesus is the Son, does not thereby retain God the Father; he loses the Father too. They mark the reason the expositors give: the Son alone reveals the Father, and the only access to the Father is through the Son; to refuse the Son is to be cut off from the Father, however much one professes to honor God. They observe the second half, supplied in the true text: “but he that acknowledgeth the Son hath the Father also” — confessing the Son brings one into possession of the Father; the two are had or lost together. They note Calvin’s judgment that this is a remarkable sentence, to be reckoned among the first axioms of religion: having confessed one true God, this second article must be added, that he is no other than the God made known in Christ. They observe Cyril’s echo that no one comes to the Father but through the Son. They note the force against the antichrists: their boast of honoring the Father while denying the Son is empty; reject the Son, and the Father is gone. The verse thus seals the doctrine: the Father is denied in denying the Son and possessed in confessing him — the truth John bids them, in the next verse, let abide in them.
24:
On 1 John 2:24 — “Let that therefore abide in you, which ye have heard from the beginning… ye also shall continue in the Son, and in the Father” — the witnesses mark the call to abide in the original gospel.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the emphasis. Robertson: “as for you” (hymeis), emphatic, contrast with the antichrists; “from the beginning” (ap’ arches). Adam Clarke: continue in the doctrines of Christ heard from the apostles. The Reformed Calvin: he exhorts to perseverance, pointing out the fruit. Wesley: if the truth abides in you, ye shall abide in the Son and the Father.

The verse calls them to hold the original truth. Let what they heard from the beginning abide, and they will abide in the Son and the Father. Perseverance in the old gospel keeps them in God.

So the witnesses see John exhort his readers, against the novelties of the antichrists, to hold fast the gospel they first received. The witnesses note the emphatic “ye” (or “as for you”) — set in pointed contrast to the seducers; whatever the antichrists do, the readers are to be different. They mark the command: “let that therefore abide in you, which ye have heard from the beginning” — the original apostolic message about the Father and the Son, the doctrine of Christ they were first taught, is to remain fixed and rooted in their hearts; they are to add no novelty and lose no part. They observe the promise attached: “ye also shall continue in the Son, and in the Father” — if the original truth abides in them, they will abide in living communion with the Son and the Father; perseverance in the doctrine secures perseverance in the fellowship. They note Calvin’s point that John exhorts to perseverance by pointing out its fruit: holding the truth keeps them in God. They observe the reciprocity: the word abiding in them, they abiding in God — the truth retained is the bond of union. They note that this is the antidote to the antichrists’ seduction: not new revelations, but the old gospel kept. The verse thus charges them to abide in what they heard from the beginning, and so to continue in the Son and the Father — the abiding that brings, the next verse adds, the promise of eternal life.
25:
On 1 John 2:25 — “And this is the promise that he hath promised us, even eternal life” — the witnesses mark the promise of eternal life.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the promise. Robertson: “the promise” (epangelia), the only instance in John’s writings; here explained as “the life eternal.” Adam Clarke: God has promised eternal life to all who believe and continue in communion. The Reformed Calvin: the best construction is “eternal life” the thing promised. Wesley: the Son hath promised us, if we abide in him.

The verse names the reward of abiding. The promise he has promised is eternal life. Continuing in the Son secures the great promise.

So the witnesses see John crown the call to abide with the great promise: eternal life. The witnesses note the link to the foregoing: those who continue in the Son and the Father (v. 24) have this for their assurance — “this is the promise that he hath promised us.” They mark the content of the promise: “even eternal life” — the supreme gift, life everlasting in fellowship with God, is the very thing promised to those who abide. They observe that this is the great encouragement to hold fast: in maintaining the truth and abiding in Christ, they have the sure promise of eternal life; in departing from it, as the antichrists did, that promise is forfeited. They note the expositors’ observation that “the promise” here is explained, as John often explains his terms, as “the life eternal” — the same eternal life that was with the Father and was manifested (1:2), now promised to believers. They observe Wesley’s qualification that the Son has promised it “if we abide in him” — the promise belongs to the persevering. They note that this sets the highest motive before them: to abide is to inherit eternal life. The verse thus seals the exhortation with its reward: the promise to those who abide is eternal life — a hope that nerves them, as the next verse shows, against those who would seduce them from it.

So the witnesses see John crown the call to abide with the great promise: eternal life. Those who continue in the Son and the Father (v24) have this for their assurance — “this is the promise that he hath promised us.” The content: “even eternal life” — the supreme gift, life everlasting in fellowship with God, promised to those who abide. This is the great encouragement to hold fast: maintaining the truth, they have the sure promise; departing, as the antichrists did, it is forfeited. The expositors note that “the promise” is explained, as John explains his terms, as the eternal life that was with the Father and was manifested (1:2). Wesley qualifies: the Son has promised it “if we abide in him” — the promise belongs to the persevering.
26:
On 1 John 2:26 — “These things have I written unto you concerning them that seduce you” — the witnesses mark the purpose of the warning.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the aim. Robertson: “them that would lead you astray” (planonton, conative present); John doing his part to rescue the sheep. Adam Clarke: the deceivers among them, who labored to pervert them. The Reformed Calvin: he writes that they might apply for the guidance of the Spirit.

The verse states why John has written. These warnings concern those who would seduce them. The aim is to guard against deceivers.

So the witnesses see John gather up the foregoing warnings by naming their purpose. The witnesses note the summary: “these things have I written unto you” — referring to the section on the antichrists and their lie (vv. 18–25). They mark the object: “concerning them that seduce you” — the false teachers who were actively trying to lead the believers astray from the truth; the present tense, as Robertson notes, marks it as an attempt in progress, the seducers at work. They observe that John writes precisely to forearm his readers against these deceivers, exposing their denial of the Son so the sheep are not led off. They note Clarke’s point that these were deceivers among them, laboring to pervert them from the faith. They observe Calvin’s sense that John writes not as though his words alone sufficed, but that they might be stirred to seek the Spirit’s guidance — the human warning and the divine anointing working together, as the next verse joins them. They note that the apostle, the faithful shepherd, will not leave his flock untaught about the wolves. They observe that the warning is an act of pastoral love. The verse thus states the purpose: John has written these things to guard them against the seducers — while reminding them, in the next verse, that the anointing within them is their abiding teacher.

So the witnesses see John gather up the foregoing warnings by naming their purpose. The summary “these things have I written unto you” refers to the section on the antichrists and their lie (vv18-25). The object: “concerning them that seduce you” — the false teachers actively trying to lead the believers astray; the present tense marks an attempt in progress, the seducers at work. John writes precisely to forearm his readers against these deceivers, exposing their denial of the Son so the sheep are not led off. Calvin’s sense: John writes not as though his words alone sufficed, but that they might be stirred to seek the Spirit’s guidance — the human warning and the divine anointing working together, as the next verse joins them. The warning is an act of pastoral love.
27:
On 1 John 2:27 — “But the anointing which ye have received of him abideth in you, and ye need not that any man teach you… the same anointing teacheth you of all things” — the witnesses mark the abiding anointing as teacher.

The Latin fathers note the abiding. Augustine: “abide in Him, that when He shall appear we may have confidence.” The grammatical-historical Robertson: “which ye received” (elabete), the anointing from Christ (v. 20). Adam Clarke: the gifts of the Spirit; the Gnostics could not prove they were divinely taught. The Reformed Calvin and Wesley: this does not exclude being taught by others who share the same anointing.

The verse assures them of the inward teacher. The anointing abides and teaches them, so they need no man to teach them. The Spirit guards them in the truth.

So the witnesses see John reassure his readers that the anointing within them is a sufficient and abiding teacher. The witnesses note the contrast with the seducers: “but the anointing which ye have received of him abideth in you” — over against the false teachers, the believers have the Spirit’s anointing (v. 20), and it remains, permanent and faithful. They mark the consequence: “ye need not that any man teach you” — they are not dependent on the seducers, nor at the mercy of those claiming superior secret knowledge; the Spirit who teaches them “of all things” is true and lies not. They observe the important qualification the Reformed and Wesleyan expositors press, lest the words be abused: John does not mean that all human teaching is needless — he is himself teaching them as he writes; rather, against the Gnostic pretenders, the anointed do not need their false instruction, for the Spirit leads into all needful truth. They note Wesley’s explicit point that this does not exclude being taught by those who partake of the same anointing. They observe the command joined: “ye shall abide in him” — the Spirit’s teaching keeps them abiding in Christ. They note Augustine’s linking of this abiding to confidence at Christ’s appearing. The verse thus assures them of the inward teacher: the abiding anointing teaches them all things, so they need not the seducers — and bids them abide in him, the very note on which the next verse closes.
28:
On 1 John 2:28 — “And now, little children, abide in him; that, when he shall appear, we may have confidence, and not be ashamed before him at his coming” — the witnesses mark the call to abide unto his appearing.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the exhortation. Robertson: “keep on abiding in him”; “if he be manifested” (ean phanerothe), a clear reference to the second coming; “confidence” (parresian), boldness. Adam Clarke: that we may have confidence, not be ashamed at his coming to judge. Wesley: O how will Jews, Socinians, nominal Christians be ashamed in that day!

The verse gathers the charge to abide. Abide in him, that at his coming we may have boldness, not shame. Abiding now prepares for confidence then.

So the witnesses see John gather his exhortations into the tender charge to abide, with an eye to Christ’s coming. The witnesses note the return to the whole company: “and now, little children” — John, having addressed the several classes, speaks again to all his beloved readers. They mark the central command, the heart of the epistle’s exhortation: “abide in him” — continue, remain, in living union with Christ; the repeated “abide” gathers up all the foregoing. They observe the motive drawn from the future: “that, when he shall appear” — a clear reference to Christ’s second coming — “we may have confidence, and not be ashamed before him at his coming.” They note the twofold prospect: those who have abided in Christ will meet him with boldness, frank and unashamed confidence; those who have not will shrink back in shame at his appearing. They observe Wesley’s vivid warning of how the unbelieving and the merely nominal will be ashamed in that day. They note the modest “we”: John includes himself among those who must abide to have confidence then. They observe that the certainty of Christ’s return makes present abiding urgent: how we meet him then depends on whether we abide in him now. The verse thus crowns the section with its great charge and motive: abide in Christ, that his coming may find us confident, not ashamed — leading, in the closing verse, to the test of righteousness as the mark of the born of God.
29:
On 1 John 2:29 — “If ye know that he is righteous, ye know that every one that doeth righteousness is born of him” — the witnesses mark righteousness as the mark of the new birth.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the inference. Robertson: “if ye know” (ean eidete), intuitive knowledge that Christ is righteous, then ye know the doer of righteousness is begotten of him. Charles Ellicott: “born of Him” shows John thinks of the Father, or the Deity in its oneness. The Reformed Calvin: the first argument that faith is connected with a holy life — we are begotten after God’s likeness.

The verse gives the family likeness. Knowing God is righteous, we know the doer of righteousness is born of him. The children resemble their Father.

So the witnesses see John close the chapter with a test of the new birth drawn from family likeness. The witnesses note the premise: “if ye know that he is righteous” — taking as known and certain that God (or Christ) is righteous, perfectly holy in his nature; the expositors note that John speaks of Father and Son so as one that the pronoun could mean either, though “born of him” points to the Father or the Deity in its oneness. They mark the inference: “ye know that every one that doeth righteousness is born of him” — from the righteousness of God it follows that whoever practices righteousness, living a holy life, must be a child of God, born of him; for the children bear the likeness of their Father. They observe Calvin’s point that this is the first of John’s arguments that faith is necessarily joined to a holy life: we are spiritually begotten after God’s likeness, so his righteousness reappears in his children. They note that the test is not perfection but the settled practice of righteousness, the habitual doing of what is right, as the evidence of the divine birth. They observe that this turns the doctrine of God’s righteousness into a searching test of sonship: the born of God resemble the God who is righteous. They note that this opens the great theme John develops in the next chapter, the children of God known by righteousness and love. The verse thus seals the chapter: knowing God is righteous, we know that the doer of righteousness is born of him — the family likeness that marks the children of God.
